Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered. I tried to set aside time after my part-time job shifts, but with limited resources at home, I often had to study at the library or coffee shops where I could focus better. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd try to physically write things out and practice problems rather than just reading. Some weeks I'd manage 15 hours, other weeks barely anything because life just got in the way.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I didn't have the luxury of a consistent schedule because I had to work to support myself. Deadlines definitely pushed me to cram, and I'd often find myself studying late at night when I finally had quiet time. I wished I could be more regular, but my circumstances made that really difficult.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were announcements or if I'd missed something important. The anxiety of falling behind was actually a big motivator for me to check in, even when I felt overwhelmed. I also logged in when I needed to participate in discussions since those counted for something.
Q4: For a while, I got into the habit of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee before work. It became almost like checking social media - just a quick scroll to see what was happening. But when things got really stressful and my anxiety spiked, that routine fell apart. The habit only lasted maybe the first month or so before everything started feeling too heavy.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually really helpful because I could learn from what other students were saying and asking. As someone who learns by doing, the interactive parts where I could engage with peers felt more meaningful than just watching videos. I also appreciated any practice exercises or hands-on activities because reading alone doesn't stick for me. The peer interactions helped me feel less alone in struggling with the material.
Q6: I often skipped the longer video lectures because I couldn't focus on them for that long, especially when I was tired from work. Some of the reading materials felt too dense and I'd tell myself I'd come back to them later, but later never really came. I also ignored some of the optional supplementary resources because I barely had time for the required stuff. When you're stretched thin, you have to prioritize.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance or if it involved actually doing something rather than passively consuming information. If I could apply what I learned to a problem or discuss it with someone, that felt valuable. Also, knowing something would be graded made me prioritize it, even if I found it boring. I needed to feel like my limited time was being spent on something that would actually help me pass.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. When you're barely keeping up with required work and dealing with high anxiety about failing, optional stuff feels almost taunting. Required activities had this weight to them - I knew I had to do them even when I didn't want to. The optional ones, even if interesting, always got pushed to the bottom of my list and usually never got done.
Q9: Yes, definitely. I downloaded a lot of materials to my phone so I could study during my commute or breaks at work without using data. I also borrowed textbooks from friends and took handwritten notes that I'd review constantly. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos that explained concepts better than the course materials. A lot of my actual learning happened away from any screen the LMS could track.
Q10: There were many times I'd be sitting with my physical notes, rewriting concepts and testing myself with flashcards I made. I'd study with a classmate at the library, quizzing each other out loud. I also did a lot of mental review while working - just going over concepts in my head during repetitive tasks at my job. None of that showed up anywhere, but it was real studying.
Q11: All the time. I'd download PDFs and lecture slides once, then never open them in the LMS again. I'd print things out at the library when I could afford it and study from paper. Sometimes I'd watch a video once to get the gist, then work from my notes instead of rewatching. My phone's storage was full of course materials that I accessed offline constantly.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. My anxiety was through the roof, and I felt like I was drowning even though I was trying. There was a period where I just felt so sad and hopeless about catching up that logging in felt pointless. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped a little, but the combination of work stress and academic pressure made it hard to even open my laptop some days.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely because I didn't know where to start. When assignments piled up at work and school simultaneously, the LMS became this source of dread rather than a learning tool. My engagement became very surface-level during those times.
Q14: Many times. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered to everything else I was stressed about. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd have materials open for an hour but actually be mentally somewhere else entirely. My body was present but my mind was dealing with anxiety and sadness.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible - like this huge mountain I couldn't climb. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel the same or worse. Some activities felt so overwhelming that avoiding them was easier than facing how behind I was. It was a terrible cycle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me act. I'd have a burst of activity right before something was due, cramming everything into a few panicked hours. The LMS probably shows these spikes of last-minute logins and submissions. Without deadlines, I honestly might not have engaged at all during my worst periods. They were stressful but necessary for me.
Q17: When I got feedback, it could go either way. Positive feedback gave me a tiny boost of hope that maybe I could do this. But negative or critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and made me want to avoid that area entirely. I appreciated detailed feedback but sometimes couldn't emotionally handle reading it right away. I'd have to wait until I felt strong enough.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so many online components to keep track of. Having 10 different courses meant I was constantly context-switching and never felt deeply engaged with any single one. The structure didn't account for students like me who need more hands-on, practical learning. I would have benefited from more structured check-ins or guided pathways through the material.
Q19: The discussion requirements actually helped because they forced me to engage with peers, which I needed. But the sheer volume of video content was hard for a kinesthetic learner like me - I learn by doing, not watching. Having more interactive elements or practice opportunities would have helped. The flexibility was both good and bad - good for my schedule, bad for my discipline.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. High activity might mean someone is trying really hard but struggling to produce quality work. Maybe they're confused and searching for answers, or anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing them. Or they could be engaging in discussions and watching videos but falling apart when it comes to actually submitting assignments. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: I can see how that happens if someone learns efficiently offline or already has strong background knowledge. Some people can read something once and understand it, so they don't need to keep logging in. That wasn't really my experience though - I was active but still struggling. But I know students who barely touched the LMS and did fine because they learned from textbooks or study groups.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're logging in, what you're clicking on, and patterns of activity over time. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement - whether you're actually learning or just going through motions. They miss offline studying, emotional state, and life circumstances. They can't tell if someone watched a video while crying from stress versus while fully focused and taking notes.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials - it's wrestling with concepts, asking questions, and trying to connect ideas. For me, real engagement happens when I'm actively doing something with the material, not passively receiving it. It also means feeling emotionally invested in your own success.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was fighting just to keep going. It can't show the anxiety attacks before logging in, or the tears when I realized I was falling behind again. It doesn't capture that I was working a job to survive while trying to study, or that I downloaded everything to study on buses and during breaks. It can't show that despite failing, I never stopped trying - I just couldn't make it work with everything else in my life.
